title: Whatever Happened to Interactive TV?
author: Lou Hirsh
abstract: E-Commerce Times reporter Lou Hirsh examines why interactive TV (ITV) failed to materialize as a mass-market category circa 2002. Aberdeen Group chief research officer Peter Kastner argues consumer time and dollars have shifted to broadband Internet and that interactive gaming from Microsoft and Sony will not require a TV set. Kastner predicts ITV capabilities must be built into future TV sets because consumers won't pay extra for set-top hardware on existing TVs.
importance_rationale: Captures the post-WebTV/AOLTV moment when ITV optimism was visibly collapsing, with Kastner offering the clearest 'built-in-only' diagnosis on record.
relevance_rationale: The 'built-in-not-bolt-on' framing maps directly onto Smart TV (Samsung Tizen, LG WebOS, Roku TV, Google TV/Chromecast Built-in) which became the dominant model 2010s-2020s. Console-as-living-room-platform also realized.
prescience_rationale: Both Kastner predictions validated: (1) ITV functions are now built into virtually all TVs sold (Smart TV penetration >80% by 2020); (2) interactive gaming did not require a TV and shifted to consoles/PCs/mobile (Xbox, PlayStation, Switch, smartphone gaming). Standalone set-top ITV (WebTV/MSN-TV/AOLTV) died as predicted.
